scene: The deciding frame of a World Championship match at the Crucible Theatre, Sheffield, between Ronnie ('The Rocket') and John Higgins. Both players have now missed chances in this frame: Higgins missed to the middle pocket earlier, and Ronnie missed a red at the right corner [450s] after what the commentator called an excellent opportunity. Despite both errors, one player has built a 41-point lead with balls still remaining [540s]; the commentator suggests that player is closing in on a 'very famous picture' -- winning the World Championship.

entities.name: John Higgins
entities.one_line: One of snooker's all-time greats; has missed to the middle pocket and is seen grimacing [477s] as the deciding frame appears to slip away from him.
entities.source_quote: John Higgins, he's been in the last two frames, John Higgins, will he get another show?

entities.name: Ronnie
entities.one_line: Referred to as 'The Rocket'; had an excellent opportunity in the deciding frame but missed the red at the right corner [450s]. One player is now described as closing in on the title.
entities.source_quote: Ronnie was in with an excellent opportunity and just lost the white a little bit and missed

entities.name: Stephen
entities.one_line: Named commentator in the booth, addressed by colleagues while analysing shot choices in the deciding frame.
entities.source_quote: Yeah, this was the shot, Stephen, wasn't it?

glossary.definition: The final frame of a match when both players are level; whoever wins it wins the match.
glossary.source_quote: just one chance in the deciding frame and this is a potential chance

glossary.definition: In major snooker matches, play is split into sessions of roughly 8 frames each, with breaks between them.
A shot where the cue ball contacts two object balls without potting either, used to nudge balls into better positions tactically.
glossary.source_quote: instead of playing it on a more exact cannon

glossary.definition: Potting a ball from a long distance across the table; high-skill, higher-risk than a short pot.
The black ball, worth 7 points, the highest-value ball on the table. Aggressive play on the black in a deciding frame is a calculated gamble.
glossary.source_quote: Both players have played aggressive shots off the black

glossary.definition: The cue ball -- the white ball the player strikes with their cue. Losing control of it means losing position for the next shot.
glossary.source_quote: just lost the white a little bit and missed the red

glossary.definition: Accidentally leaving yourself unable to see the ball you need to hit next, caused by your own shot rather than the opponent's.
glossary.source_quote: just snooker yourself on the red

narratives.title: Both players missed; one now leads by 41 and closing in
narratives.summary: Ronnie missed a red at the right corner [450s] after an excellent opportunity, and Higgins had missed earlier. Despite both errors, one player has built a 41-point lead in the frame [540s] with balls still on the table. The commentator describes them as closing in on 'a very famous picture' [600s], implying a World Championship win is imminent.

narratives.title: Remarkable comeback from 8-3 down
narratives.summary: One player trailed 8-3 across sessions and looked certain to lose; the trailing player played what the commentator calls unreadable snooker to claw back to a deciding frame. The turnaround surprised even the commentators.

narratives.title: Two all-time greats, sudden death
narratives.summary: The commentator explicitly frames this as a match between two all-time greats, adding weight to the tension. Even at the highest level, a deciding frame remains unpredictable.

key_moments.what: Ronnie named; commentator confirms both players have a chance in the deciding frame
key_moments.why_it_matters: Neither player had yet taken control; the frame was genuinely open.

key_moments.what: Both players played aggressive shots off the black, both left tricky positions
key_moments.why_it_matters: The black is the highest-value ball; attacking it in a deciding frame reflects the high-stakes urgency.

key_moments.what: John Higgins misses his shot to the middle pocket
key_moments.why_it_matters: Missing a makeable pot in a deciding frame can hand the match to the opponent.

key_moments.what: Another chance described for John Higgins; commentator reads his body language as knowing
key_moments.why_it_matters: Higgins appears to sense he has left himself in difficulty, hinting his chance may have passed.

key_moments.what: Commentator says Higgins might just have played his last shot
key_moments.why_it_matters: In snooker this means the opponent may have a clear run to the colours needed to win, without Higgins returning to the table.

key_moments.what: Commentator calls both players all-time greats, notes anything can happen in a deciding frame
key_moments.why_it_matters: Frames the occasion: two of the sport's greatest ever players, sudden death, no lead is safe.

key_moments.what: Ronnie misses a red at the right corner after an excellent opportunity
key_moments.why_it_matters: Even Ronnie -- widely considered one of the greatest potters in history -- could not escape the pressure of the deciding frame.

key_moments.what: A grimace from John visible at the table
key_moments.why_it_matters: Body language suggesting frustration or resignation; mirrors the earlier read on Higgins's situation.

key_moments.what: Commentator notes both players paid the penalty for their shot choices
key_moments.why_it_matters: Confirms neither player took clean control; errors on both sides shaped the frame.

key_moments.what: Scoreboard shows one player 41 points in front with balls still on the table
key_moments.why_it_matters: A 41-point lead in a single frame is significant; the trailing player would need their opponent to miss to have a realistic chance.

key_moments.what: Commentator says the pink was the much easier shot to get onto the red
key_moments.why_it_matters: Illustrates how fine the margins are: choosing one ball over another can determine the outcome of the entire frame.

key_moments.what: Commentator says the leading player is 'closing in on a very famous picture'
key_moments.why_it_matters: Winning the World Championship is one of snooker's most celebrated achievements; this signals the match may almost be over.
